title: ‘Strongest evidence’ yet that vaping probably causes cancer
html_body:
What matters in cancer research |
View this email in your browser Thursday 2 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we learn that vaping is a likely cause of oral and lung cancers, hear that an influential, 49-year-old article on asbestos and talcum powder has been retracted and discover that tumours have an unexpected food source.
E-cigarettes were first introduced in the early 2000s and their long-term effects on cancer rates are still unknown. (whitebalance.space/Getty)

Vaping probably causes cancer

Nicotine-containing vapes are likely to cause cancer in humans, just like they do in mice, according to a review of case reports, mouse studies and human biomarker studies from 2017 to 2025. This is “by far the strongest evidence” that vapes likely cause oral and lung cancer, says cancer researcher Bernard Stewart who co-authored the study. Around 23% of mice that inhale e-cigarette vapor later develop lung cancer. Many compounds in vapes have been shown to be carcinogenic, including formaldehyde, benzene and nickel. People who vape have elevated levels of toxic chemicals in their urine and have tissue injury, oxidative stress markers and DNA damage that are associated with tumours. There are two case reports of people who have developed oral cancer after chronic e-cigarette use.

ABC News | 7 min read
 Reference: Carcinogenesis paper (30 March)

49-year-old asbestos article retracted

The Lancet has retracted an influential commentary article published in 1977 that claims asbestos in talcum powder is not a health hazard and does not cause cancer. Two public health historians, David Rosner and Gerald Markowitz, discovered that the article’s anonymous author was actually Francis Roe, a paid consultant working for talcum-powder manufacturer Johnson & Johnson and that Roe had sought feedback from the company prior to publication. “This unsigned commentary has been used to justify the continued marketing of cosmetic talc for nearly half a century,” write the two historians.

Retraction Watch | 7 min read
 Reference: The Lancet correspondence and retraction notice (25 March)

In the news

  • The US Food and Drug Administration has sent a warning letter to the biotechnology company ImmunityBio in relation to false and misleading claims made about its bladder cancer treatment, Anktiva. The company’s co-owner Patrick Soon-Shiong made the unfounded suggestion that Anktiva could treat all cancers on a podcast and in television ads. (STAT | 4 min read)
  • The pharmaceutical company Merck will acquire Terns Pharmaceuticals for around US$6.7 billion, bringing Tern’s oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor for chronic myeloid leukemia onboard. (Genetic Engineering & Biotechnology News | 7 min read)
  • Hundreds of people were harmed by a breast-surgery service at a UK hospital that missed cancers, performed mastectomies that might have been unnecessary and failed to provide chemotherapy, according to an independent review of County Durham and Darlington Foundation Trust by the Royal College of Surgeons. (BBC | 4 min read)

Must-read papers

Cancer’s unexpected food source

Tumours can use an abundant chemical called glutathione as a nutrient source by breaking it down into its constituent amino acids, which are then used to build proteins and other molecules. Researchers slowed tumour growth in mice by blocking the activity of an enzyme that cleaves glutathione to release the amino acids.“These results reveal an actionable pathway of nutrient acquisition in cancer with direct therapeutic implications,” write the authors.

Reference: Nature paper (18 March)
The extracellular chemical glutathione (Glu) can be broken down by the enzyme γ-glutamyltransferase (GGT) to generate the amino acid cysteine, which is then used by cancer cells to make proteins and other key molecules. (Hecht, F., Zocchi, M., Tuttle, E.T. et al./Nature)

Features & opinion

Agent Orange finding could help veterans

Researchers have confirmed that people who were exposed to the herbicide Agent Orange during the Vietnam War are at greater risk of developing blood cancers called myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS). Currently, the US government doesn’t recognize Agent Orange as a cause of MDS, curtailing benefits for veterans with this condition. The finding could help veterans with blood cancer access disability compensation from the government, says haematologist-oncologist and study co-author Mikkael Sekeres. “We’ve long suspected that [Agent Orange] could lead to MDS, but the military and no formal study had ever made the connection before,” says Sekeres.

STAT | 6 min read
 Reference: Blood Advances paper (24 February)

Non-coding RNA’s role in cancer

The vast majority of the human genome doesn’t code for proteins and was long thought of as ‘junk DNA’. Researchers now know that these non-coding sequences have a range of purposes: forming microRNA that regulate genes, circular RNA that seem to soak up regulatory molecules, piRNA that defend against jumping genes and small interfering RNA that attack specific messenger RNA. These mRNA strands can also promote or suppress cancer. MicroRNA-21, for example, is overexpressed by some cancers, including glioblastoma. A circular RNA called MALAT1 is linked with several types of cancer and is overproduced by tumours.

Knowable Magazine | 10 min read

Infographic of the week
Researchers have found that injecting a virus called oncolytic alphavirus M1 (OVM) into mice suppresses the growth of glioblastoma, a lethal brain cancer, and also reverses the cancer’s suppression of the immune system. (Cellular & Molecular Immunology | 14 min read) (Dieli, C., Di Simone, M., Dieli, F. et al./Cell. Mol. Immunol.)

Quote of the week

“Thinking is not your friend.”

Actor Amanda Peet used this advice from a therapist after she was diagnosed with stage I breast cancer. She received her diagnosis while both of her parents were in hospice — and rumination became impossible to avoid. She admires people who can “sit with uncertainty in matters of life and death” but she’s not one of them. (The New Yorker | 27 min read - paywall)

I’ve noticed that I sleep better when I spend more time outside. So, I was curious to read that exposure to red light might cause our mitochondria to produce more energy molecules known as ATP, and that red light is being promoted as a therapy for a range of diseases, including cancer. Does red light also help with sleep?

Tell me about your light diet and send tips and feedback to cancerbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Felicity Nelson, editor, Nature Briefing: Cancer
With contributions by Laura Woodrow

items.title: Vaping probably causes cancer

People who vape have elevated levels of toxic chemicals in their urine and have tissue injury, oxidative stress markers and DNA damage that are associated with tumours. There are two case reports of people who have developed oral cancer after chronic e-cigarette use.

Currently, the US government doesn’t recognize Agent Orange as a cause of MDS, curtailing benefits for veterans with this condition. items.title: Access Nature and 54 other Nature journals

summary.overview: 이번 이슈는 비관음이 암과 관련된 최신 연구, 아스베스토스 논문 철회, 베타리움 관련 병역 지원, 비코딩 RNA의 암 역할, 그리고 Nature 저널 구독 기회 등 다양한 과학 소식을 다룹니다.
covered_item_titles: 베타리움 노출로 인한 병역 지원 확대

article_briefings.title: 비관음이 암 유발 가능성 확정
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 니코틴 함유 비관음이 인간에서 암을 유발할 가능성이 높다는 연구 결과가 발표됐다. 2017년부터 2025년까지의 사례 보고서, 쥐 실험 및 인간 생물마커 연구를 바탕으로 '가장 강력한 증거'로 평가받고 있다.

article_briefings.title: 49년 된 아스베스토스 논문 철회
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 1977년 발표된 '아스베스토스가 탈록파우더에 포함될 경우 건강 위협이 아님'을 주장한 논문이 철회됐다. 두 공중보건 역사학자가 해당 논문의 신뢰성 부족을 지적하며 재검토를 촉구했다.

article_briefings.title: 베타리움 노출로 인한 병역 지원 확대
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 베트남 전쟁 중 베타리움에 노출된 전우들이 혈액암인 골수종증후군(MDS) 위험이 높다는 연구 결과가 발표됐다. 미국 정부는 현재 해당 질병을 전우 복지 지원 대상으로 인정하지 않고 있다.

article_briefings.title: 비코딩 RNA가 암에 미치는 영향
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 인간 유전자의 대부분이 단백질을 만들지 않지만, 비코딩 RNA가 마이크로RNA와 원형 RNA 형태로 유전자 조절 및 세포 기능에 중요한 역할을 한다는 연구가 발표됐다.

article_briefings.title: Nature 및 54개 저널 구독 기회
article_briefings.briefing_ko: Nature와 54개의 Nature 계열 저널에 대한 구독 서비스가 제공되며, 과학 연구자 및 학생들에게 최신 학술 정보 접근을 지원합니다.
